summary: Joel Spolsky highlights a Best Buy employee's confession about being taught to scam customers into unwanted subscriptions, using it as evidence of a systemic problem he calls 'Econ 101 management.' He argues that major consumer-facing companies rely on crude metrics rather than thoughtful training, which incentivizes frontline workers to deceive customers. The post connects this specific scandal to a broader pattern across American consumer electronics, ISPs, telecoms, and cable companies.
key_points: Best Buy employees were actively taught by managers to trick customers into signing up for subscriptions like MSN
Companies substitute simplistic metrics for genuine investment in training frontline workers
When low-wage, high-turnover workers are given targets without proper guidance, they inevitably resort to scamming customers
This pattern is systemic across American consumer electronics, ISPs, telecoms, credit card companies, and cable companies
money_quotes: Instead of having smart people figure out how to train their frontline customer service workers to serve customers well and profitably, they make up metrics that sound good and let the low wage, high-turnover customer service people come up with their own systems, which, inevitably, involve scamming customers and ripping them off.
key_insight: When companies substitute crude metrics for thoughtful training of frontline workers, the inevitable result is that employees game the system by scamming customers.
